reasoning: From the examples, a horizontal line is a formula; a short vertical stroke on the left edge of a line is ¬; a vertical branch down from a line adds an antecedent (if … then …). Multiple branches from the same line are nested conditionals, equivalent to conjoined antecedents. Reading the M1 diagram: the top line (consequent) is A; it has one antecedent which is the whole line B, so antecedent → A. That antecedent line B itself has two antecedents: one is C; the other is a conditional with antecedent ¬E and consequent D. Thus B is C → ((¬E → D) → B’s consequent), where B is the consequent of that inner nest. Putting it together yields (C → ((¬E → D) → B)) → A.
i. x is a galaction. In the prose, this is the only predicate that also appears negated (“not a galaction”), matching the single negated leaf in the diagram, so we assign it to A.
ii. the polyverse is Groop‑normal. This global condition sits as the outermost antecedent in the diagram, so we assign it to F.
iii. x is dingly. In the text it appears only as the consequence of the clause with ¬(galaction), which in the diagram is the branch fed by the negated A; assign D to dingly.
iv. x has a satetorope. It pairs with “pulsoid” as a second antecedent that, together, lead toward galaction; thus map the satetorope predicate to C.
v. x is a quaxor. One branch in the diagram directly implies galaction independently of the pulsoid/satetorope branch; that matches “quaxor → galaction,” so assign E to quaxor.
vi. x is a pulsoid. It is the other antecedent paired with “has a satetorope” in the path leading to galaction; assign B to pulsoid.

answer: A horizontal content line is a formula; the leftmost ‘judgment’ stroke merely asserts it. A short vertical tick on the left edge of a content line negates that formula (¬). A vertical branch from a line down to another line adds an antecedent: if (lower line) then (upper line). Several branches from the same line are nested conditionals, equivalent to conjoining antecedents. Disjunction and conjunction are expressed via → and ¬: p ∨ q is ¬p → q, and p ∧ q is ¬(p → ¬q).
reasoning: Summarizes how to read every symbol needed to interpret the provided examples and to translate the given diagrams.
